title: Queen Platform Bed Frame
summary: Solid ash platform bed with bolted, knock-down joinery — so it can actually leave the room it was built in.
description:
Most shop-built beds are a nightmare to move. This one bolts together with barrel nuts, so it comes apart flat in ten minutes and reassembles rock-solid. That single decision is worth more than any joinery flourish.

The frame carries the mattress on slats — no box spring. Ash is the pick here: it's stiff, it's cheap relative to oak or walnut, and it takes a beating. Maple or oak substitute directly.

The rails are the structural heart. Everything else is trim.

tools.note: Cutting long rails to length accurately. A circular saw and a track will do.
Mortises, or use a drill press and chisels.
Barrel nut cross-holes must be drilled dead perpendicular or the bolt won't catch.
You need long ones — 60"+ to clamp across the frame.

materials.name: Ash, 8/4, for legs and rails
materials.note: ~$7/bd ft. The rails need thick stock — they carry the whole load.

materials.name: Ash, 4/4, for headboard panel and slat ledgers
Poplar or pine, 4/4, for slats
materials.note: Nobody sees the slats. Don't spend ash money on them.

materials.name: Bed bolt / barrel nut hardware kit
materials.note: Two per rail-to-leg joint. This is what makes the bed knock-down. Do not substitute pocket screws.

materials.name: Clear interior finish (hardwax oil or wipe-on poly)
materials.note: A bed gets touched every day. Use a finish that repairs easily.

cutList.note: Laminate two pieces of 8/4 to get 3" thickness.
Replace the two head-end legs with these full-height posts.
Glued and screwed inside the bottom of each side rail. The mattress load lands here.
Spaced ~3" apart. A queen mattress needs slats no more than 3" apart or foam sags between them.

steps.title: Mill all stock — then let it sit
steps.body:
Mill everything to final dimension, then **stack it with stickers and leave it two or three days.** Freshly milled stock moves as internal stress releases. Building immediately means building with wood that's still changing shape.

This costs you nothing but patience and saves you a twisted rail.

steps.title: Laminate the legs and posts
steps.body:
Glue up 8/4 pairs to get 3" square legs. Clamp well, cure overnight, then mill the laminated blanks square.

The two head-end legs are full-height headboard posts (40"). The foot legs are short (14").

steps.title: Cut the rail tenons
steps.body:
Each rail gets a tenon on both ends: 1/2" thick, 4" long, centered, shouldered all around.

These tenons are for **alignment**, not strength — the bolts carry the load. But a sloppy tenon means a bed that racks, so cut them to fit snug by hand pressure.

steps.title: Chop the leg mortises
steps.body:
Mortise each leg to receive its rail tenons: 1/2" wide, 4" tall, 4-1/4" deep. Router with an edge guide, or drill-press-and-chisel.

Dry fit every joint. The frame should stand up on its own, square, before any hardware goes in.

steps.title: Drill for the bed bolts
steps.body:
**The step to get right.** Drill a bolt clearance hole through the outside face of each leg, through the mortise, and into the rail tenon.

Then drill the cross-hole in the tenon for the barrel nut — **on the drill press, dead perpendicular.** If that hole is off by more than a couple degrees, the bolt will not thread into the barrel nut and you will be fishing around inside a rail with a flashlight, cursing.

Test every joint with the actual hardware before you finish anything.
materials: Bed bolt / barrel nut hardware kit

steps.title: Build the headboard
steps.body:
Groove the inside faces of the headboard posts and the head rail. Slide the panel slats in dry — **do not glue them.** A 58"-wide panel of solid ash will expand and contract across the seasons by nearly 1/4". Glue it and it will crack.

Let it float. That's the whole trick to solid-wood panels.
materials: Ash, 4/4, for headboard panel and slat ledgers

steps.title: Attach the slat ledgers
steps.body:
Glue and screw a 3/4" x 1-1/2" ledger along the inside bottom edge of each side rail. Screw every 8" — this joint carries the mattress, the sleepers, and everything that happens on the bed.

Don't rely on glue alone here.
materials: Ash, 4/4, for headboard panel and slat ledgers

steps.title: Sand and finish before assembly
steps.body:
Sand to 180. Finish every part **while they're still separate** — you will never reach the inside corners once it's bolted together.

Three coats of hardwax oil or wipe-on poly, sanding lightly at 320 between coats.
materials: Clear interior finish (hardwax oil or wipe-on poly)

steps.title: Assemble in the bedroom
steps.body:
Carry the parts in flat and bolt it together where it's going to live. Ten minutes with a hex key.

Drop the slats in loose, spaced ~3" apart. Don't fasten them — loose slats are quieter and let you pull one to vacuum under the bed.

When you move house, it comes apart the same way.
materials: Bed bolt / barrel nut hardware kit
